content.thinking:
The user wants to:
1. Remove the CredibilityBar component entirely (the "NIST Standardized · Built in Public · zknox Collaboration · Open Source" section)
2. Fix the "Falcon Spec" link in the footer - the current link points to `https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/206/final` which is FIPS 206, which is actually the Falcon spec (now called FN-DSA). Let me check - actually FIPS 206 is the ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) standard. Falcon was standardized as FN-DSA, but I'm not sure of the exact FIPS number. 

I'm reviewing the NIST post-quantum cryptography standards to verify the correct FIPS number for Falcon - FIPS 203 covers ML-KEM, FIPS 204 is ML-DSA, FIPS 205 is SLH-DSA, and FIPS 206 should be FN-DSA (Falcon), though I need to confirm if it's been finalized.

Since the user flagged the Falcon specs link as broken, I should use the official Falcon specification from https://falcon-sign.info/ rather than relying on a potentially incomplete NIST standardization URL.

content.text: Now let me check what the correct Falcon spec URL should be, since the current one may be broken.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "NIST FIPS 206 FN-DSA Falcon specification 2025 2026"

Links: [{"title":"FIPS 206: FN-DSA (Falcon) | CSRC","url":"https://csrc.nist.gov/presentations/2025/fips-206-fn-dsa-falcon"},{"title":"FIPS 206 Status Update Ray Perlner Computer Security Division NIST","url":"https://csrc.nist.gov/csrc/media/presentations/2025/fips-206-fn-dsa-(falcon)/images-media/fips_206-perlner_2.1.pdf"},{"title":"Quantum-Ready FN-DSA (FIPS 206) Nears Draft Approval from NIST | DigiCert","url":"https://www.digicert.com/blog/quantum-ready-fndsa-nears-draft-approval-from-nist"},{"title":"Decoding NIST PQC Standards: What They Are, What’s Final, and What’s Next | Encryption Consulting","url":"https://www.encryptionconsulting.com/decoding-nist-pqc-standards/"},{"title":"draft-turner-lamps-cms-fn-dsa-00 - Use of the FN-DSA Signature Algorithm in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)","url":"https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-turner-lamps-cms-fn-dsa/"},{"title":"FIPS 206 Status Update","url":"https://groups.google.com/a/list.nist.gov/g/pqc-forum/c/1HXzjlMUU6Y"},{"title":"NIST Releases First 3 Finalized Post-Quantum Encryption Standards | NIST","url":"https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2024/08/nist-releases-first-3-finalized-post-quantum-encryption-standards"},{"title":"Quantum-ready FN-DSA (FIPS 206) nears draft approval - Data Centre & Network News","url":"https://dcnnmagazine.com/build/quantum-computing/quantum-ready-fn-dsa-fips-206-nears-draft-approval/"},{"title":"Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) Standardization - 2025 Update","url":"https://postquantum.com/post-quantum/cryptography-pqc-nist/"},{"title":"draft-ietf-cose-falcon-03 - FN-DSA for JOSE and COSE","url":"https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-cose-falcon/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about NIST FIPS 206 FN-DSA Falcon:

## Overview

FN-DSA is NIST's official name for the digital signature scheme based on FALCON, selected alongside ML-DSA and SLH-DSA for post-quantum standardization. FN-DSA is short for FFT (fast-Fourier transform) over NTRU-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm.

## Technical Details

FN-DSA is a lattice-based signature scheme in the Hash-Then-Sign paradigm where the signature is a lattice point close to a target point derived from a randomized message hash. FN-DSA has very small signatures and public keys but is difficult to implement.

FN-DSA was chosen specifically to address use-cases where signature size is at a premium, with FN-DSA-512 signatures about 666 bytes and public keys around 897 bytes (FN-DSA-1024 uses 1280 bytes).

## Timeline and Status

On August 28, 2025, NIST submitted the draft standard for FN-DSA (FIPS 206) for approval. The release may coincide with the NIST PQC Standardization Conference in late September 2025, and if past timelines are any guide, the public review will last about a year, with the final standard expected in late 2026 or early 2027.

## Implementation Considerations

FALCON's signing complexities make FN-DSA less suited for leaf certificates, which are signed frequently. Instead, it shows strong potential for FN-DSA in root and intermediate certificates.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

NIST Releases First 3 Finalized Post-Quantum Encryption Standards | NIST

results:
Based on the search results, here's what I found about NIST FIPS 206 FN-DSA Falcon:

## Overview

FN-DSA is NIST's official name for the digital signature scheme based on FALCON, selected alongside ML-DSA and SLH-DSA for post-quantum standardization. FN-DSA is short for FFT (fast-Fourier transform) over NTRU-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm.

## Technical Details

FN-DSA is a lattice-based signature scheme in the Hash-Then-Sign paradigm where the signature is a lattice point close to a target point derived from a randomized message hash. FN-DSA has very small signatures and public keys but is difficult to implement.

FN-DSA was chosen specifically to address use-cases where signature size is at a premium, with FN-DSA-512 signatures about 666 bytes and public keys around 897 bytes (FN-DSA-1024 uses 1280 bytes).

## Timeline and Status

On August 28, 2025, NIST submitted the draft standard for FN-DSA (FIPS 206) for approval. The release may coincide with the NIST PQC Standardization Conference in late September 2025, and if past timelines are any guide, the public review will last about a year, with the final standard expected in late 2026 or early 2027.

## Implementation Considerations

FALCON's signing complexities make FN-DSA less suited for leaf certificates, which are signed frequently. Instead, it shows strong potential for FN-DSA in root and intermediate certificates.

content.thinking: The FIPS 206 draft page returned a 404 too. Let me try the falcon-sign.info page which is the original Falcon specification website.

content.text: Let me check the original Falcon spec page.

input.prompt: What is this page? Is it the Falcon digital signature specification page? What URLs are available for the spec?

content.content:
# Overview

Yes, this is the official Falcon digital signature specification page. It serves as the primary resource for the cryptographic algorithm submitted to NIST's Post-Quantum Cryptography Project.

## Available URLs for the Specification

The page provides direct links to specification documentation:

1. **Main Specification (PDF):** `falcon.pdf` - The formal specification document
2. **Submission Package (ZIP):** `falcon-round3.zip` - Contains the specification, source code, scripts, and test vectors
3. **Reference Implementation (ZIP):** `Falcon-impl-20211101.zip` - Updated source code archive
4. **Browsable Source Code:** `impl/falcon.h.html` - Online code viewer

## Key Information

The page describes Falcon as "a cryptographic signature algorithm submitted to NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography Project on November 30th, 2017." It emphasizes the algorithm's advantages in security, compactness, speed, and RAM efficiency for post-quantum applications. Performance benchmarks and historical submission packages from earlier rounds are also available.

file.content:
import { Footer } from "@/components/landing/Footer"
import { Hero } from "@/components/landing/Hero"
import { NavHeader } from "@/components/landing/NavHeader"
import { TheProblem } from "@/components/landing/TheProblem"
import { WhyStarknet } from "@/components/landing/WhyStarknet"
import { PerformanceStats } from "@/components/landing/PerformanceStats"
import { CredibilityBar } from "@/components/landing/CredibilityBar"
import { PlaygroundSection } from "@/components/interactive/PlaygroundSection"
import { PipelineSection } from "@/components/interactive/PipelineSection"
import { AccountDeploySection } from "@/components/interactive/AccountDeploySection"
import { ScrollReveal } from "@/components/ScrollReveal"

export default function Home(): React.JSX.Element {
  return (
    <main className="min-h-screen">
      <NavHeader />
      <Hero />
      <ScrollReveal>
        <TheProblem />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <ScrollReveal>
        <WhyStarknet />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <ScrollReveal>
        <PerformanceStats />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <CredibilityBar />
      <ScrollReveal>
        <PlaygroundSection />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <ScrollReveal>
        <PipelineSection />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <ScrollReveal>
        <AccountDeploySection />
      </ScrollReveal>
      <Footer />
    </main>
  )
}

content.text:
Done. Here's what I changed:

1. **Removed CredibilityBar** — deleted the import and `<CredibilityBar />` usage from `page.tsx`
2. **Fixed Falcon Spec link** — the old URL (`csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/206/final`) returned a 404 since FIPS 206 hasn't been finalized yet. Updated it to `https://falcon-sign.info/` (the official Falcon specification page).

The `CredibilityBar.tsx` file still exists but is no longer imported anywhere — you can delete it if you want.
